english: Red and brown rice mala bowl

food_subgroup: Mixed grain rice dish with spiced sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Ma La Xiang Guo Rice; FOODON_00002678 (rice dish) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a base of mixed white, red, and brown rice with quinoa topped with a ma la stir-fried sauce and variable protein and vegetable components.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite: HPB Singapore Foodo mixed rice dish data; USDA FoodData Central quinoa and brown rice entries; peer-reviewed ma la sauce composition literature
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Ma La Xiang Guo Rice. Values estimated by compositing: (1) HPB Foodo mixed rice dish (white + brown rice base, ~55% of dish by weight), (2) USDA FoodData Central cooked quinoa (NDB 20137, ~10% of dish), (3) ma la sauce estimated from published Sichuan spiced oil-based sauce composition (~35% of dish). Weighted average applied per 100 g. Sodium elevated due to sauce. This is a medium-confidence estimate; exact restaurant formulation not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database mixed rice entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ma La Xiang Guo Rice. GI estimated at 58 based on: (1) white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database), (2) brown rice GI ~50 (University of Sydney GI Database), (3) quinoa GI ~53 (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Weighted composite for a mixed grain base (~70% white rice, ~20% brown rice, ~10% quinoa) yields estimated GI ~65 for the grain base; the presence of fat and fibre from the ma la sauce and vegetables is expected to lower the effective GI to approximately 58. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 94.0) / 100 = 54.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 94.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from ma la sauce (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ma La Xiang Guo Rice due to its very high sodium load from the spiced sauce, which can substantially exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load per serving (~54.5) due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Ma La Xiang Guo Rice in reduced portions; the mixed grain base (red rice, brown rice, quinoa) moderates the glycemic response compared to plain white rice, but the large serving size produces a high glycemic load that requires careful carbohydrate management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content, partially offset by whole grain components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content of the ma la sauce is the primary concern; the inclusion of brown rice, red rice, and quinoa provides beneficial dietary fibre and unsaturated fats, but portion control and sauce reduction are advised to manage overall sodium and caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ma La Xiang Guo Rice (麻辣香锅饭, Má Là Xiāng Guō Fàn) is a Singaporean restaurant adaptation of the Sichuan Chinese dish 'mala xiang guo' (numbing-spicy fragrant pot), served over a mixed grain rice base. The dish originates from Sichuan cuisine, where 'ma' (麻) refers to the numbing sensation from Sichuan peppercorns and 'la' (辣) refers to the heat from dried chillies. In the Singapore context, this version is distinguished by its enriched rice base comprising white rice, red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, providing additional dietary fibre, protein, and micronutrients compared to plain white rice. The sauce is a stir-fried spiced oil blend typically containing Sichuan peppercorns, dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, doubanjiang (fermented chilli bean paste), and cooking oil, which imparts a bold, aromatic, numbing-spicy flavour profile. Toppings and protein components vary by restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~5.8 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre (~1.9 g per 100 g) from the multigrain base. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the sauce (~420 mg per 100 g, approximately 1680 mg per standard 400 g serving). The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), moderated by the brown rice, red rice, and quinoa components. This dish is relevant for dietary counselling in hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management, particularly regarding sodium and carbohydrate load.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for Ma La Xiang Guo Rice as served at this restaurant. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing approved source data for the constituent grain components (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) and published ma la sauce composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for white rice, brown rice, and quinoa. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and based on approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Sodium is estimated conservatively from typical ma la sauce formulations and may be higher in restaurant preparations. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the composite source entries used. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this dish is predominantly plant-based in its grain and sauce components and B12 is not reported in the primary source entries. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source entries. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero based on plant-oil-based sauce composition; no hydrogenated fats reported. 8. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero for the grain and plant-based sauce components; variable if animal protein is added. 9. GI set to 58 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for white rice (GI 72), brown rice (GI 50), and quinoa (GI 53), weighted by approximate grain composition and adjusted downward for fat and fibre from sauce. 10. Halal status not confirmed for this specific restaurant preparation; sauce may contain non-halal ingredients. 11. Allergen profile is indicative; soy is likely present in doubanjiang-based sauces.
